Narrative:
Non-Reactive - Individual is considered to be not immune to infection with HBV. Equivocal - Unable to determine if anti-HBs is present at levels consistent with immunity. The individual should be further
assessed by associated risk factors and the use of additional diagnositc information, or another sample may be collected and
tested.
Reactive - Anti-HBs concentration detected at >10 mIU/mL. Individual is considered to be immune to infection with HBV. Results may be falsely decreased if patient taking Biotin.

Comment:
No response to M tuberculosis antigens detected.
Infection with M tuberculosis is unlikely, but high risk
individuals should be considered for additional testing
(ATS/IDSA/CDC Clinical Practice Guidelines, 2017). The
reference range is an Antigen minus Nil result of <0.35 IU/mL. Chemiluminescence immunoassay methodology

Narrative:
Non-Reactive - Individual is considered to be not immune to infection with HBV. Equivocal - Unable to determine if anti-HBs is present at levels consistent with immunity. The individual should be further assessed
by associated risk factors and the use of additional diagnositc information, or another sample may be collected and tested. Reactive - Anti-HBs concentration detected at >10 mIU/mL. Individual is considered to be immune to infection with HBV. Results may be falsely decreased if patient taking Biotin. Acknowledged by: Ryan, Renee G, PA-C on 07/20/23 0810

QuantiFERON Criteria Comment — — LABCORP
Comment:
QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus is a qualitative indirect test for
M tuberculosis infection (including disease) and is intended for use
in conjunction with risk assessment, radiography, and other medical
and diagnostic evaluations. The QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus result is
determined by subtracting the Nil value from either TB antigen (Ag)
value. The Mitogen tube serves as a control for the test. QUANTIFERON TB1 AG VALUE 0.03 IU/mL — LABCORP
QUANTIFERON TB2 AG VALUE 0.01 IU/mL — LABCORP
QuantiFERON Nil Value 0.00 IU/mL — LABCORP
QuantiFERON Mitogen Value >10.00 IU/mL — LABCORP

Patient Instructions (continued)
None · Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: · Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body
· Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding
· Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye
· Sudden severe headache with no known cause
· Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination
It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get
emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a
stroke. 
For more information:
American Stroke Association
1-888-4-STROKE
www.strokeassociation.org

None · Call 911 or have someone take you to the Emergency Department if you have any of the following: · Sudden numbness or weakness of your face, arm or leg especially on one side of the body
· Sudden confusion, difficulty speaking or trouble understanding
· Changes in your vision or loss of sight in one eye
· Sudden severe headache with no known cause
· Sudden dizziness, trouble walking, loss of balance or coordination
It is important to seek emergency care right away if you have further stroke symptoms. If you get
emergency help quickly, the powerful clot-dissolving medicines can reduce the disabilities caused by a
stroke.

Admin Instructions: Give IV push if: Maternal systolic blood pressure drops below 100 mmHg or 10%
drop from baseline
Fetal heart rate deceleration (20 beats per minute below baseline
lasting more than 30 seconds) occurs with in 20 minutes of epidural 
dosing
Discuss dosing with Anesthesia in patients with pre-eclampsia, hypertension, cardiac history or tocolytic

Ordering Provider: Terrassa, Margarita, MD Status: Discontinued (Past End Date/Time)
Ordered On: 01/29/21 0223 Starts/Ends: 01/29/21 0315 - 01/30/21 2137
Ordered Dose (Remaining/Total): 2-20 milli-units/min (—/—) Route: Intravenous
Frequency: Titrated Ordered Rate/Order Duration: 2-20 mL/hr / —
Admin Instructions: Start at 2 milli-units/min and titrate up or down by 2 milli-units every 30 min to a max of 20 milli-units/min. Titrate to
maternal/fetal tolerance, with a goal of achieving an adequate labor pattern defined by progressive cervical effacement and cervical
dilation of approximately 0.5 cm/hr to 1 cm/hr once active labor is achieved. Contractions should not be more frequent than every 2
minutes. Notify physician for tachysystole.

[DOB-1507] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
L&D Delivery Note (continued)
Findings: Amniotic Fluid clear
Uterus normal
Tubes and ovaries normal bilaterally
Infant:
Gender: Viable male infant
Weight: 3695 g (8 lb 2.3 oz)
Apgars: 4 @ 1 minute /
8 @ 5 minutes
Indication for C/Section: Failure to Progress Procedure Details: The patient was taken to the operating room where epidural
anesthesia was found to be adequate. She was prepped and draped in the usual sterile
manner in the dorsal supine position with leftward tilt. A Pfannenstiel incision was made
and carried down to the fascia. The fascia was incised in the mid-line and extended
transversely with Mayo scissors. The fascia was grasped and elevated and separated
from the underlying rectus muscles superiorly and inferiorly. The peritoneum was
identified and entered. Peritoneal incision was extended superiorly and inferiorly with
good visualization of the bladder. The bladder blade was inserted and the vesicouterine
peritoneum was incised transversely and the bladder flap was created digitally. The
bladder blade was reinserted. The lower uterine segment was incised in a transverse
fashion. The head was elevated and delivered through the incision. The remainder of
the infant was delivered without difficulty. The umbilical cord was clamped and cut and
the infant was handed off the field. Cord ph and cord bloods were obtained. The placenta 
was removed intact and appeared normal. The uterine incision was closed with running
locked sutures of 0 monocryl. The abdominal cavity was irrigated and cleared of all clot
and debris. The uterine incision was inspected and noted to be hemostatic. Rectus
muscles were reapproximated in the midline with 0 chromic. The fascia was closed with
0 PDS in running continuous fashion. The subcutaneous tissue was closed with 3-0
chromic. The skin was closed in subcuticular fashion with 4-0 Monocryl.
Instrument, sponge, and needle counts were correct prior the abdominal closure and at
the conclusion of the case. Mother and baby to recovery room in stable condition. Complications: none

[DOB-1495] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
This note was copied from a baby's chart. Mother has some blistering on both nipples, reports some pinching with latch. Infant currently latched to left nipple, upon release nipple lipstick shaped. Discussed the importance of asymmetrical latch and how to achieve deep latch. Assisted mother with football positioning on right side, demonstrated deep asymmetrical latch. Mother reports no pain
or pinching with latch. Discussed how to determine nutritive sucks and audible swallows noted. Encouraged mother to
provide support and guidance to nipple for infant to achieve proper latch. Discussed when to expect milk to come in
and ways to tell infant is getting enough. Encouraged mother to call for assistance or questions as needed.
This note was copied from a baby's chart. Mother reports that she has been able to feed infant a few times this afternoon and the latch is feeling better. She
denies any additional nipple damage. Encouraged mother to call for assist as needed.
This note was copied from a baby's chart. P1 term baby. Mom getting ready for D/C. She reports her breast are feeling fuller now and baby is nursing better. She
denies any questions. Discussed how to know baby is getting enough milk and discussed OPLC, given card.

4000 KRESGE WAY

MRN: [PHONE-1494], DOB: 11/7/2000, Legal Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-1493] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Dr Lewis to bedside after RN request for MD to review strip. MD discussed with patient the need for IUPC placement
and amnioinfusion to resolve variable decelerations. MD also voiced concern about size of baby and slow cervical
change. MD mentioned possibility of cesarean section if labor does not progress. Patient states she really does not
want to have a c section. MD agreeable to allowing labor to continue and reassess cervix in a couple of hours.
Late entry:
OR case started at 1848. FOB was told that we will observe moment of silence as much as possible upon delivery of
baby. FOB verbalized understanding that if there were any complications with baby, there would not be absolute
silence. Birth time was at 1855. Baby was still on abdomen and FOB tried to walk around to the sterile field after being
asked by AA (Dr Lammers) to stay seated. I asked the FOB to sit down as well and he pushed his body toward me to
get me to move. I put my hands in front of my body to keep him away from me. Baby went directly to warmer for resuscitative measures (see notes on delivery record) where three nurses including
NNP were assisting in care. FOB was getting loud and trying to push his way toward baby in order to whisper his
prayer in baby's ear. I told him that baby needed assistance breathing at the moment and that he can sit right where
he was (less than 1 foot away from baby) and say his prayer. FOB tried to push his way to warmer which is when I put
my hands in front of my body again and told him he will have to leave the OR if he does not sit down. Dr Lammers
stood up and at the same time Dr Pennell came into OR. FOB sat down. FOB stayed seated after this but continued to interrupt resuscitative efforts by making rude comments toward staff and
doctors. Breathing efforts toward baby continued until 10 minutes. FOB said more than one prayer during this time
with no attempt at interruptions of prayer by any staff.
FOB signed visitor behavior contract with security officer; Stating that going forward any aggressive, threatening
behavior or interference with pt or infant care he will be removed from the hospital and not allowed back during the pts
stay. House Manager, Risk and MB Unit Manager made aware.
Nursing Note
Electronically signed by Hicks, Kara, RN at 01/30/21 0410
Electronically signed by McClish, Sarah, RN at 01/31/21 0749
Electronically signed by Hurst, Megan Rachelle Parker, RN at 01/31/21 0542

Anderson, Claire Elise, RN at 1/31/2021 0731
Author: Anderson, Claire Elise, RN Service: Obstetrics Author Type: Registered Nurse
Filed: 01/31/21 0929 Date of Service: 01/31/21 0731 Creation Time: 01/31/21 0731
Summary:Father's behavior in OR
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 85

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 KRESGE WAY
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-283], DOB: 11/7/2000, Legal Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-1491] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Late entry
Summary of day
Patient called out requesting information about epidural at 1100. This RN explained the epidural process. Patient
states she would like an epidural at this time. Patient's significant other states " Do not give it to her unless I tell you. She doesn't really want it." This RN explained to the patient's significant other that it is the patient's choice and that I
will get anesthesia if the patient asks for an epidural. He proceeded to tell me "leave the room because it's not your
decision." The patient states that she was okay to discuss with her significant other. Dr. Sherman was at bedside at 1128 for epidural placement. The patient's significant other was told to take a seat
during the procedure and he responded " I wont pass out, I've killed people before so I'm not sitting down." At 1634, Dr. Terrassa was at bedside to discuss plan of care with patient and significant other. MD discussed risks and
benefits of cesarean delivery. The patient's significant other stated " We will not be doing a c-section, I believe in God
over science." 
At 1645 the patient's significant other came out to the nurses station to talk with this RN in the hallway. The patient's
significant other told this RN that they would refuse the cesarean section if we could not promise a minute of silence at
delivery. Discussed with significant other normal protocols for our staff in a c-section and why it is important for us to
be able to vocalize amniotic fluid color, baby birth time, bleeding, etc but that we would try to limit these to
accommodate for a moment of silence for prayer. This RN told the significant other that we could not promise that
because each delivery is different and we can not predict how the baby will come out at delivery and if he would need
special attention due to patient risk factors. The significant other then proceeded to say that "we will risk the baby and
mom's life if I can not say a prayer to my son and the room cannot be silent. My son's first words that he hears will not
be coming from you all." This RN told him we would do everything we can to make that possible but can not promise. At 1655 The patient's significant other stated to this RN " If we do the c-section I do not think the patient needed any
other numbing medicine." This RN explained that the c-section is abdominal surgery and we would have to give her
stronger medicine so she was comfortable during the procedure. He proceeded to say " My family has addiction
problems and you will not make my baby addicted to any medications." This RN told him that is part of the c-section
and that I can get anesthesia to the room to talk about medications if they decide to proceed with the c-section. At 1659 the patient called out and said she would like to proceed with a cesarean delivery at this time. This RN again
explained that we would try to have a minute of silence at delivery but could not promise at this time. Also explained
no video in the OR was allowed and Significant other would have to be seated during the entire procedure behind the
sterile drape. He would not be allowed to stand or go into the sterile field. Both patient and significant other agreed to
this. 
Cancel discharge. Telephone order per Sara Canchola, APRN.
Electronically signed by Anderson, Claire Elise, RN at 01/31/21 0929

Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care
Outcome: Ongoing, Progressing
Goal Outcome Evaluation:
Plan of Care Reviewed With: patient
Goal Outcome Evaluation:
Meeting goals. VSS. Pt. Claims good pain relief with medicines. Incision healing well. No s/s of infection. No falls. Pt. Showing good bonding with infant.

Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-305], DOB: 11/7/2000, Legal Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-306] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Clinical Notes (group 1 of 2) (continued)
Tolerating labor well, sleeping between contractions. IUPC placed around 3AM due to recurrent variable decels, now
improved s/p amnioinfusion. At 3AM cervix was 3/90/0. UCs not yet adequate so working to titrate pitocin. EFW last
week per u/s was 8lb5oz so did discuss potential for cephalopelvic disproportion. Will continue to titrate pitocin and
once adequate if no further progress this AM discussed need to consider cesarean delivery
Lauren Lewis, MD
Reviewed in detail noted slow progress of labor increased risk of PP bleeding due to over saturation of uterine
receptor for prolonged labor and increased risk of infection due to ROM >24. Also, noted moderate caput and suspect
Cephalopelvic Disproportion and rec to proceed with CD to avoid bleeding complications in light of clinically labor not
progressing and now seeing decrease variability in FHT's. Pt given time to ask questions and will like to discuss with
FOB who mentioned that "he does not believe in science and he will like to let God decide". Also, that having a CD will
not allow him to do what he will like to do/ Reviewed in detail that I even if she is having minimal cervical change I
suspect that patient will have failure to descend in light of pelvic exam and progression of labor/
BH Louisville
Cesarean PROGRESS NOTE
Patient Name: Anella Noble
DOB: 11/7/2000
MRN: [PHONE-307]
Post-Op Day 1 S/P C-Section
Delivered a male infant. Subjective
Patient reports:
Pain is well controlled. This with foley catheter in place. Ambulating without difficulty. Tolerating po. Lochia normal. The patient plans to breastfeed.

Discharge Planning Assessment

Patient Name: Anella Noble MRN: [PHONE-1455]
Today's Date: 2/2/2021
Admit Date: 1/28/2021
Discharge Needs Assessment
No documentation. Discharge Plan
Row Name 02/02/21 1037
Plan
Plan Infant to discharge home with mother when medically ready. Deena Green, CSW
Plan Comments Mother: Anella Noble, MRN [PHONE-321]; Infant: AnellasBoy “Alik” Noble, MRN
[PHONE-322]. CSW was consulted for “Social support,” and “hx of abuse per
report, family dynamics concerns.” Of note, no urine toxicology obtained on
mother nor infant. Cord toxicology was not sent. No indication of need for
toxicology screens at this time. CSW spoke with RN Mary Jude M, who advised
that father is controlling and loud, but not violent or threatening. CSW met with
mother alone at bedside. Mother verified address, phone, and insurance. Mother reports she has spoken with MedAssist about adding infant to insurance
coverage. Mother reports that she and father are in the process of moving, but
mother did not disclose new address. Mother reports they have car seat, crib, clothes, diapers and other supplies. Mother is not current with WIC, is breast
feeding. CSW educated mother about WIC program. Mother plans on follow up
with Dr. Kahttah and is comfortable scheduling appointments and will have
transportation to appointments. Mother reports that maternal grandmother is
involved and supportive. Mother denies any violence, threats, or feeling unsafe. HANDS program discussed with mother, mother agreeable to HANDS referral.
Electronically signed by Price, Leigh T., MD at 02/01/21 0832

Acknowledged: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0054 for Placing Order Culver, Amy Christine, RN 01/30/21 2309 for D/C Order
Admin instructions: Give IV push if:
Maternal systolic blood pressure drops below 100 mmHg or 10% drop from
baseline
Fetal heart rate deceleration (20 beats per minute below baseline lasting
more than 30 seconds) occurs with in 20 minutes of epidural dosing
Discuss dosing with Anesthesia in patients with pre-eclampsia, hypertension, cardiac history or tocolytic
Caution: Look alike/sound alike drug alert Dilute with NS to 5-10 mg/mL. Central line preferred, if unavailable use large bore IV access with
frequent nurse monitoring of IV site. Package: 76014-005-25

oxytocin in sodium chloride (PITOCIN) 30 UNIT/500ML infusion solution [342791827] (Discontinued)
Electronically signed by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD on 01/30/21 1308 Status: Discontinued
Mode: Ordering in Telephone with readback mode Communicated by: Sylvia, Kayla, RN
Ordering user: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0223 Ordering provider: Terrassa, Margarita, MD
Authorized by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD Ordering mode: Telephone with readback
Frequency: Routine Titrated 01/29/21 0315 - 2 days Class: Normal
Discontinued by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD 01/30/21 2137
Acknowledged: Sylvia, Kayla, RN 01/29/21 0443 for Placing Order Culver, Amy Christine, RN 01/30/21 2309 for D/C Order
Admin instructions: Start at 2 milli-units/min and titrate up or down by 2 milli-units every
30 min to a max of 20 milli-units/min. Titrate to maternal/fetal
tolerance, with a goal of achieving an adequate labor pattern defined by
progressive cervical effacement and cervical dilation of approximately 0.5
cm/hr to 1 cm/hr once active labor is achieved. Contractions should not be
more frequent than every 2 minutes. Notify physician for tachysystole. Package: 63323-012-30, 0338-0049-03

Admin instructions: Do not exceed 4 grams of acetaminophen in a 24 hr period. Max dose of 2gm
for AST/ALT greater than 120 units/L
If given for pain, use the following pain scale:
Mild Pain = Pain Score of 1-3, CPOT 1-2
Moderate Pain = Pain Score of 4-6, CPOT 3-4
Severe Pain = Pain Score of 7-10, CPOT 5-8

Order comments: NO TREATMENT RECOMMENDED IF: 1) Maternal GBS Status Known Negative 2) Scheduled Cesarean Birth
With Intact Membranes, Not in Labor 3) Maternal GBS Status Unknown, No Risk Factors TREAT WITH ANTIBIOTICS IF: 1)
Maternal GBS Status Known Positive 2) Maternal GBS Status Unknown With Risk Factors: a) Previous Infant Affected By GBS
Infection b) GBS Urinary Tract Infection (UTI) or Bacteriuria During Pregnancy c) Unexplained Maternal Fever (100.4F (38C) or
Greater) During Labor d) Prolonged Rupture of Membranes (18 or More Hours) e) Gestational Age Less Than 37 Weeks

Order comments: Indwelling Urinary Catheter Removal Criteria Discontinue Indwelling Urinary Catheter Unless One of the Following
is Present: Urinary Retention or Obstruction Chronic Urinary Catheter Use End of Life Critical Illness with Strict I/O GU Tract or
Abdominal Surgery Stage 3/4 Sacral / Perineal Wound Required Activity Restriction: Trauma Required Activity Restriction: Spine
Surgery If Patient is Being Followed by Urology Contact Them PRIOR to Removal Do Not Remove Indwelling Urinary Catheter Order
is Present with a CLINICAL REASON to Maintain the Catheter. Provider is Required to Include a Clinical Reason to Maintain a

Authorized by: Grider, Ann R., MD Ordering mode: Standard
Frequency: Routine 02/03/21 - Class: Clinic Performed
Quantity: 1
Order comments: Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if
you are not taking percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 lbs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for heavy 
vaginal bleeding more than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. Follow up with Women First in 6 weeks. The office should contact you
to make appointment within a week of discharge. Transfer Patient [343176071] (Completed)
Electronically signed by: Terrassa, Margarita, MD on 01/30/21 1929 Status: Completed

Intervention: Identify and Manage Fall Risk (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Perform standard risk assessment with a validated tool or comprehensive approach appropriate to
the patient on admission; reassess fall risk frequently, with change in status or transfer to another
level of care.
Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team. Determine need for increased observation, equipment and environmental modification, such as low 
bed and signage, as well as supportive, nonskid footwear. Adjust safety measures to individual developmental age, stage and identified risk factors. Reinforce the importance of safety and physical activity with patient and family. Perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain assessment, personal needs, including assistance with toileting.
Intervention: Prevent Skin Injury (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess skin risk on admission and at regular intervals throughout hospital stay. Keep all areas of skin (especially folds) clean and dry. Maintain adequate skin hydration. Relieve and redistribute pressure and protect bony prominences; implement measures based on
patient-specific risk factors. Match turning and repositioning schedule to clinical condition. Encourage weight shift frequently; assist with reposition if unable to complete independently. Float heels off bed. Avoid pressure on the Achilles tendon. Keep skin free from extended contact with medical devices. Use aids (e.g., slide boards, mechanical lift) during transfer.
Intervention: Prevent and Manage VTE (venous thromboembolism) Risk (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess for VTE risk. Encourage/assist with early ambulation.
Initiate and maintain compression or other therapy, as indicated based on identified risk in
accordance with organizational protocol/provider order. Encourage both active and passive leg exercises while in bed, if unable to ambulate.
Intervention: Prevent Infection (Completed)

Care Plan (continued)
Goal: Optimal Comfort and Wellbeing (Resolved)
Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Goal: Readiness for Transition of Care (Resolved)
Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Maintain skin and mucous membrane integrity; promote hand, oral and pulmonary hygiene. Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance.
Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices). Evaluate ongoing need for invasive devices; remove promptly when no longer indicated. Outcomes

Intervention: Monitor Pain and Promote Comfort (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess pain level, treatment efficacy and patient response at regular intervals using a consistent
pain scale.
Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain. Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Intervention: Provide Person-Centered Care (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG

Intervention: Maintain Anesthesia/Analgesia Safety (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Optimize patient position for insertion accuracy (e.g., sitting, lateral decubitus); consider hip wedge
to displace uterus and avoid vessel compression. Maintain fetal heart rate monitoring.
Implement safety measures (e.g., verify solution/flow rate/pump setting when infusion starts, dose
changes or shift changes, use portless infusion tubing, use pumps that prevent free-flow, recheck/trace tubing connections, label tubing). Prepare for intravenous fluid bolus administration prior to initiation of anesthesia to avoid
hypotension.
Prepare for induction of neuraxial anesthesia/analgesia agent; advocate for test dose and
anticipate reversal agent. Maintain supportive environment; promote relaxation (e.g., offer reassurance, provide information, answer questions). Anticipate urgent medical management for hypotension, block at high level or medication toxicity. Verify intact catheter tip with removal.

Care Plan (continued)
Problem: Breastfeeding
Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Goal: Effective Breastfeeding (Resolved)
Dates: Resolved: 02/02/21
Disciplines: Nurse, Interdisciplinary
Continue uterine fundal massage if fundus remains boggy. Encourage early breastfeeding. Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for
measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL). Verify bladder has been emptied; encourage voiding, perform catheterization if unable to void. Anticipate need for vaginal exam to determine presence of lacerations or hematoma. Watch for changes in blood pressure, heart rate and urine output. Anticipate administration of uterotonic medication. Prepare for additional bleeding-control measures (e.g., balloon tamponade, vaginal packing). Anticipate rapid infusion of intravenous fluid and blood product administration. Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed. Monitor for coagulopathy and signs of abnormal bleeding (e.g., oozing from intravenous sites, petechiae).
Anticipate need for surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine compression
sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy). Maintain calm, reassuring approach with patient and family; answer questions; provide information
and timely responses to assist with anxiety reduction.
Outcomes
Date/Time User Outcome
02/02/21 1053 Meyer, Mary J, RN Met
02/01/21 2357 Parrott, Kimberli, RN Progressing
Intervention: Promote Breast Care and Comfort (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Optimize nipple and skin integrity; avoid use of alcohol or soap solutions; utilize topical agents
such as colostrum, breast milk. Mutually establish acceptable comfort level; evaluate pain using consistent tool or self-report
description. Encourage use of nonpharmacologic comfort measures, such as warm or cold applications, breast
massage, manual expression of milk and use of well-fitting support bra; offer analgesia if needed. Monitor and manage signs of breast or nipple infection; advocate for treatment of mother and
infant if nipple is infected. Encourage continued breastfeeding or breast pumping, even when pain, engorgement, blocked
duct or an infection is present; provide reassurance and guidance. Encourage adequate maternal hydration, nutrition and rest.
Intervention: Promote Effective Breastfeeding (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Mutually develop a feeding plan; include father/partner/support person. Promote cue-based and infant-led feeding; goal of 8 to 12 times per 24-hour period. Provide ongoing support during feeding sessions to enhance maternal infant experience. Adjust environment to provide calm surroundings and privacy; limit distractions. Assist with recognition of and response to infant feeding readiness and satiety cues; demonstrate
infant rooting and sucking reflexes. Demonstrate techniques to maintain infant’s feeding readiness state, such as unwrapping and
gentle tactile or vocal stimulation. Assist with effective positioning, latch and breast attachment techniques (e.g., move infant to
breast versus breast to infant, stimulate suck with drop of colostrum or breast milk). Observe feedings at minimum twice daily, assessing infant’s ability to latch, suck and swallow, mother infant positioning and presence of milk transfer; evaluate infant for signs of adequate intake 
(e.g., number of wet diapers and stools). Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 369

Intervention: Support Exclusive Breastfeeding Success (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Discuss and reinforce benefits for infant and mother.
Initiate uninterrupted breastfeeding within 1 hour of birth; promote skin-to-skin contact. Advocate for patience and persistence; encourage breastfeeding in the presence of difficult
circumstances; eliminate or minimize separation of infant-mother and encourage rooming-in.
Initiate pumping of breast milk within 6 hours of birth if unable to nurse. Assist with breast pumping, proper handling and storage of breast milk. Advocate for the use of alternative breast milk feeding methods when breastfeeding is medically
not possible (e.g., cup, finger, feeding tube device). Discourage use of a pacifier until breastfeeding is well-established. Assess support system integrity; emphasize the importance of father/partner/support person
involvement and use of available resources. Discuss future feeding plans and assist with barrier resolution (e.g., return to work, birth control).

Intervention: Minimize Heavy Bleeding/Hemorrhage (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Facilitate position change, if possible, to enhance maternal circulation and increase uterine blood
flow (lateral position with hip wedge). Maintain large-bore intravenous access; provide fluid resuscitation as indicated by maternal 
status.
Continue fetal heart rate monitoring until delivery. Note presence of bleeding when membranes are ruptured; test for presence of fetal blood. Assess bleeding amount; check underpads for presence of blood pooling; weigh pads for
measurement accuracy (1gm equals 1mL). Verify readiness and accessibility of blood products if transfusion needed. Administer oxygen therapy in the presence of maternal hypoxia. Prepare for expedited delivery with change in maternal or fetal status. Prepare for uterotonic medication administration for bleeding management following delivery. Anticipate need for further surgical intervention with uncontrolled bleeding (e.g., uterine
compression sutures, vessel ligation or embolization, hysterectomy). Maintain calm, reassuring approach with patient and family; answer questions; provide information
and timely responses to assist with anxiety reduction.

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Protect and secure insertion site; observe for signs of infection, sepsis or line dislodgement. Provide meticulous dressing and catheter care to decrease risk of infection; maintain closed
system.
Optimize fluid balance, nutrition, sleep and glycemic control to maximize infection resistance.
Identify potential sources of infection early to prevent or mitigate progression of infection (e.g., wound, lines, devices). Obtain cultures prior to initiating antimicrobial therapy. Do not delay treatment for laboratory results 
in the presence of high suspicion. Administer ordered antimicrobial therapy promptly; reassess need regularly. Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures. Note: Maternal fever can occur during neuraxial analgesia/anesthesia that is not associated with
infection. Source of temperature elevation should be investigated. Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 372

Intervention: Prevent or Manage Infection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Verify Group B streptococcus status and presence of known infection. Prepare to administer antimicrobial therapy prophylaxis within 60 minutes prior to delivery, unless
there is current coverage provided by existing antimicrobial therapy regimen. Note: If cesarean delivery is urgently needed, prophylaxis should be administered as soon as
possible following incision. Assist with obtaining cultures, as indicated. Limit digital vaginal exams or invasive vaginal procedures, especially after membranes are
ruptured.
Notify neonatal team for delivery. Prepare to send placenta for histology following delivery, as indicated. Provide fever-reduction and comfort measures, as needed.

Intervention: Support Labor Pain Coping and Management (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 01/30/21
Description: Determine pain management plan with patient and caregiver; review plan regularly. Use a consistent, validated tool for pain assessment; evaluate pain level, effect of treatment and
patient’s response at regular intervals; along with standard pain scales, the patient can also be
asked how she is coping with the pain. Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain. Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response. Prepare for initiation of analgesia/anesthetic, such as neuraxial or inhaled; patient-controlled
neuraxial analgesia may be used. Monitor and address medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting, maternal
respiratory depression and neonatal depression.
Initiate individualized nonpharmacologic pain management measures, including massage and
effleurage, hot or cold application, acupressure, visual imagery, breathing techniques, birthing ball, shower or hot tub. Encourage frequent position changes; use pillows to support position. Maintain calm environment, including low lighting and decreased stimulation.

Use cold application, as culturally-appropriate, to the incisional area for the first 24 to 48 hours to
enhance comfort. Encourage early ambulation, when able, to help avoid gas accumulation which can add to
discomfort. Verify correct infant latch when breastfeeding to prevent nipple pain. Consider the presence and impact of preexisting chronic pain. Encourage patient and caregiver involvement in pain assessment, interventions and safety
measures.
Individualize pharmacologic pain management plan; titrate medication to patient response. Combine multimodal analgesia and nonpharmacologic strategies to help potentiate synergistic
effects and enhance comfort (e.g., complementary therapy, diversional activity). Offer around-the-clock dosing of pain medication to keep pain levels in control. Manage medication-induced effects, such as constipation, nausea, vomiting. Support and optimize psychosocial response to pain.
If engorgement occurs, encourage more frequent breastfeeding or pumping and storing additional
milk to ease discomfort. Note: Cold compresses, as culturally–appropriate, may be used if bottle-feeding.
If post-dural puncture headache identified, encourage adequate hydration and anticipate the need
for epidural blood patch.
If hemorrhoids are present and painful, offer topical pain relief and sitz baths for comfort.
Outcomes

Intervention: Identify and Manage Contributors (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Reassess fall risk frequently and with change in status or transfer to another level of care. Communicate fall injury risk to interprofessional healthcare team. (e.g., rounds change of
shift/provider, patient transport). Anticipate needs; perform regular intentional rounding to assess need for position change, pain
assessment, personal needs (e.g., toileting) and placement of necessary items. Promote use of personal vision and auditory aids (e.g., glasses, hearing aids). Assess assistance level required for safe and effective self-care; provide support as needed (e.g.,
toileting, mobilization). Define behavior and activity limits to patient and family to decrease fall or drop risk.
If fall occurs, assess for and treat injury; determine cause; revise fall injury prevention plan. Regularly review medication contribution to fall risk; consider polypharmacy and high-risk
medications (e.g., antiepileptic, narcotic, regional block, anesthesia, sedation) given within last 24
hours.
Adjust medication administration times to minimize risk of falling. Balance adequate pain management with potential for oversedation
Intervention: Promote Injury-Free Environment (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Provide a safe, barrier-free environment that encourages independent activity. Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 377

Description: Promote aspiration prophylaxis (e.g., positioning). Minimize use of opioid medication; consider alternatives. Consider complementary therapy (e.g., acupuncture point P6 stimulation, aromatherapy, controlled

treatment.

preoperative fasting, cool cloth).

Intervention: Support Parent Role Transition (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Develop trust, relationship and rapport. Use a family-focused approach; promote involvement of support system in infant care.
Incorporate cultural beliefs, rituals and practices in family-centered care. Encourage and support breastfeeding, frequent holding and skin-to-skin contact with newborn. Encourage attachment behaviors; promote involvement in infant care. Monitor maternal emotional state, as well as maternal-infant interaction. Encourage and answer questions; share resources for support following discharge.

monitor for signs of hypoventilation.

collapse/obstruction and aspiration. Stimulate patient to increase arousal and respiratory effort. Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 379

Intervention: Optimize Skin Protection (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Reassess skin injury risk and inspect skin frequently (e.g., scheduled interval, with turning, with
change in condition) to provide optimal prevention. Maintain adequate tissue perfusion (e.g., encourage fluid balance, avoid crossing legs, constrictive
clothing or devices) to promote tissue oxygenation. Maintain head of bed at lowest degree of elevation tolerated, considering medical condition and
other restrictions. Limit amount of time head of bed is elevated greater than 30 degrees to prevent
friction/shear injury. Avoid positioning onto an area that remains reddened. Minimize incontinence and moisture (e.g., toileting schedule; moisture-wicking pad, diaper or
incontinence collection device, skin moisture barrier). Cleanse skin promptly and gently when soiled utilizing a pH-balanced cleanser. Relieve and redistribute pressure (e.g., schedule position changes; utilize higher specification foam 
mattress, chair cushion, constant low-pressure or alternating-pressure support surface; medical
device repositioning; protective dressing applicatio
Support increased progressive functional activity (e.g., therapeutic exercise) to decrease risk
associated with immobility. Balance rest with activity.
Intervention: Promote and Optimize Oral Intake (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 02/02/21
Description: Assess need and provide assistance with meal set-up and feeding. Adjust diet or meal schedule based on preferences and tolerance. Minimize unnecessary dietary restrictions to increase oral intake. Offer oral supplemental foods or drinks to enhance calorie and protein intake. Establish bowel elimination program to increase comfort and appetite. Provide and encourage oral hygiene to enhance desire to eat. Consider nutrition support if oral intake remains inadequate.

Intervention: Monitor and Manage Uterine Activity (Completed)
Frequency: Per CPG
Dates: End: 01/30/21
Description: Monitor uterine contraction activity and fetal heart rate during labor process. Facilitate maternal position change to lateral with hip wedge as necessary. Prepare to administer intravenous fluid bolus. Anticipate need to adjust or discontinue pharmacologic therapy (e.g., cervical ripening agent, oxytocin).
Prepare for oxygen administration in the presence of maternal hypoxia. Prepare to administer tocolytic agent if uterine activity fails to normalize. Reassess uterine activity and fetal heart rate for response to interventions. Prepare for delivery if contraction pattern or fetal heart rate fail to normalize or with change in
maternal-fetal status. Notify neonatal team of delivery.

Point: Code H (Resolved)
Point: Infection Prevention (Resolved)
Staff and visitors should use hand cleanser or wash their hands when entering and exiting a room, as well as prior to and after
touching the patient. Visitors should wear proper protection when infection-prevention signs are posted. Speak up if infection prevention principles are not practiced by patients, visitors or healthcare team members. Friends and family with symptoms of illness should avoid visiting. Educate patients and family members about the importance of daily hygiene care and special handling of drains, wounds and
dressings.

Point: Fall Prevention (Resolved)
Instruct the patient and family to call for help with activity to prevent a fall, even if feeling able to do things independently. Keep the room and pathway to bathroom clutter-free. Caution patient and family not to disconnect or silence any alarms.

Patient Education (continued)
Title: Perinatal Fall Injury Risk Education (Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Overview (Resolved)
Point: Pain Assessment Process (Resolved)
It is important that pain is managed, so that the patient can be an active participant in the treatment and therapy needed for their 
recovery.
Inform the patient and family that managing pain is a partnership. It is important to speak up if pain is not well-controlled.
Point: Risk Factors (Resolved)
- change in balance, gait, mobility due to pregnancy weight gain, obesity
- fatigue or sleep disturbance
- hypotension
- inappropriate footwear
- prolonged bedrest - history of fall - medication (e.g., polypharmacy; sedative, hypnotic, laxative, diuretic, antihyperglycemic, cardiac)
- depression
- anxiety
- fear of falling
- impaired vision, hearing
- environmental factor (e.g., inadequate lighting, slippery or uneven surfaces, clutter, hard to reach objects)
- stairs without railings
- unfamiliar surroundings
- neurologic disease
- injury that affects gait, mobility
Description:
Determine the patient's preferred pain scale and discuss planned reassessment schedule. Patient Friendly Description:

Baptist Health Louisville

[DOB-979] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Risk factors may include: · poor balance due to pregnancy weight gain
· the size of your belly (uterus) · being unable to see your feet · wearing shoes with high heels or flip-flops
· having fallen in the past · trouble sleeping, feeling tired
· effect of medicine you are taking
· being afraid of falling or losing balance
· having a sickness or injury that causes dizziness or weakness
· not being able to see or hear well · having dangers at home, such as poor lighting, slippery or uneven floors, clutter · having to reach high or bend low to get what you need
· taking the stairs
Standing up too fast may make your blood pressure drop and cause you to feel dizzy. Being on bedrest for a long time can cause your muscles to get weak.
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
Point: Medication Management (Resolved)
- analgesics
- sedatives
- antipsychotics
- cardiac
Patients should be aware of medication side effects. Provider's instructions should be followed for taking medication. Provider should be notified of any side effects.

Point: Safety (Resolved)
- nonskid, supportive shoes should be worn (e.g., low heels, no flip flops)
- caution should be used while walking; watch for trip danger
- avoid carrying small children, if possible
- avoid carrying large loads
- use caution when using stairs; use railings for support - use caution getting in or out of the bath or shower
Following delivery: - assistance getting out of bed should be requested, as needed
- walking and carrying the infant is strongly discouraged. The infant should be placed back into the bassinet/crib
- presence of a support person should be encouraged following delivery
- discourage holding or feeding infant if feeling sleepy or has been sedated
Point: Understanding Your Risk for Falls (Resolved)

[DOB-985] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
A drain may be put in your bladder because: · all the pee (urine) won't come out · something is stopping or blocking the pee (urine) from coming out · a test is needed to find out how well your kidneys or bladder are working
· you need to find out how much you are peeing (passing urine) · you are having a long surgery or test · you can't get up to the bathroom to pee (immobility) · you have open sores in your private area that need to heal
Topic: Catheter-Associated Infection (Resolved)
Signs you may have an infection include:
- accurate measurement of urine in critically ill patient - prolonged surgical procedure
- receiving large volumes of infusions or diuretics during surgery
- medications that increase urine output - open sacral or perineal wounds with incontinence
- required prolonged immobilization
- select genitourinary surgical procedures
- required intraoperative monitoring of urinary output
Point: Signs/Symptoms (Resolved)
- fatigue
- fever
- flank pain
- cloudy, foul-smelling urine
- hematuria
- suprapubic tenderness
- urinary frequency
- urinary urgency
- dysuria

[DOB-989] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Title: Cesarean Delivery Education (Obstetrics) ()
Title: Postpartum (Cesarean Delivery) Education (Obstetrics) ()
Taking the tube out as soon as you don't need it will help. Other ways to prevent infection include: · keep the tube fastened to your upper leg to keep it from being pulled out by mistake
· make sure it drains well · make sure there are no breaks in the drainage tube
· use a very clean way to get a sample of pee (urine) if needed
· keep the drain bag below your hips and off the floor
It will be emptied often to check how much urine is in the bag. Everyone should wash their hands before and after touching your tube. Your private area should be washed at least once a day and after you poop.
Topic: Elsevier Education Documents (Resolved)
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
- hung below the level of hips, but never on the floor
The care team will: - wash hands before and after catheter care
- provide perineal care at least daily and after bowel movements
Point: Cesarean Delivery (Resolved)
Point: Self-Care (Resolved)
This includes taking care of the incision. Ambulating soon after delivery promotes healing and improves wellbeing. When getting up from bed, the patient should sit briefly and then stand without walking for a few seconds. This will help to
prevent dizziness or lightheadedness. Good posture should be maintained when walking to decrease back pressure.

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 KRESGE WAY
Louisville KY 40207-4605
Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-990], DOB: 11/7/2000, Legal Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-991] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
It can take several weeks to feel fully physically recovered. Encourage rest each day and to gradually increase activity. Suggest napping when the infant naps. Visitors and social activities should be limited. Strenuous work should be avoided. Lifting restrictions should be followed, as instructed by healthcare provider. Help should be sought from family and friends for household chores and child care. Personal hygiene includes: - avoiding douching and using tampon use until care provider says that it is okay
- using a squirt bottle for rinsing perineum front to back after urinating or having a bowel movement; wiping should also be from
front to back
- showering regularly once care provider has said that it is okay
Point: Expected Physical Changes (Resolved)
Uterine changes: - menstrual-like cramping is expected as uterine involution takes place
- lochia should progressively decrease in amount; change in color is normal over the next several weeks
- lochia changes from rubra (1 to 3 days) to pinkish-brown and gradually to alba (white) after postpartum day 10 and for up to 6
weeks. It should never have a foul odor (may be a signal of infection)
Breast changes: - breast engorgement occurs on postpartum day 2 or 3
- if not breastfeeding, breasts should return to prepregnant condition within 1 to 2 weeks after delivery
Fluid shifts: - fluid shifting following delivery may cause bladder overdistension. Voiding may also be affected by anesthetic
- voiding should occur as soon as possible after delivery; if unable to void, intermittent catheterization may be necessary; with
continued difficulty, a temporary indwelling urinary catheter may be necessary
- sweating and more frequent urination occurs due to normal fluid shifts during the first week after delivery
Constipation: - it is common for bowel movements to be irregular after delivery

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 KRESGE WAY

Noble, Anella
MRN: [PHONE-992], DOB: 11/7/2000, Legal Sex: F
Adm: 1/28/2021, D/C: 2/3/2021
[DOB-993] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
You will notice many changes over the next few days as your body returns to a before-pregnancy state. Changes include: · cramps, like what you feel during a period, as your uterus shrinks a little each day
· bleeding that will gradually slow and stop
· the bleeding (discharge) will also change in color from red to pinkish-brown to white
· the bleeding or discharge should never smell bad
· you will notice your breasts become swollen and firm with breast milk, this is called engorgement and
happens 2 or 3 days after birth; a snug bra and ice packs may help you feel better until it gradually
goes away
You may have a tube in your bladder to drain urine for a short while after your cesarean delivery. This will be
removed within the first few hours after delivery.
It is important that you are able to pee (urinate) soon after delivery.
If you have trouble peeing you may have to have a catheter (tube in your bladder) put in for a short time. You may notice that you are going to the bathroom (to pee) more often or sweating more during the first
week after birth. This is normal because your body is getting rid of extra fluid from pregnancy.
It is common to have trouble pooping (having a stool) after your baby is born. Drink a lot of fluids, especially water. Eat foods that are high in fiber like fresh fruits, raw vegetables, whole grain breads and cereals. Your doctor may suggest medicine to help you go if needed.
- stool softeners can help to make the stool easier to pass
- drink 8 to 10 8-ounce glasses of water each day
- eat more high-fiber foods, like fruits, vegetables, whole grain breads, cereals and dry beans
- a mild laxative may be ordered if no bowel movement has passed in a couple of days
Point: Infant Safety (Resolved)
To prevent accidental dropping or injury, the baby should be placed safely in the bassinette when napping and to sleep at night. The infant should not be fed unattended if patient is feeling sleepy or if sedated from pain medicine, especially when
breastfeeding or practicing skin-to-skin contact. Extra care should be taken when bathing the infant as they can easily slip.

Description:
An infant could fall or be dropped at any time when being held, carried or being passed from one person to another. Risks
are higher following cesarean birth, when using pain medications, placing the infant skin-to-skin and during breastfeeding. Determine with the parent/caregiver a plan to decrease risk, including the use of siderails.

[DOB-995] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
The birth of your baby causes emotions that may be new to you. Some parents feel a strong attachment to their baby right away. For others it may take longer. Both are
normal.
Bonding is a process that happens while you are giving your baby the care that they need every day. You
may not even know it is happening until your baby smiles for the first time and you feel great love and joy.
It is very common to feel down or cry easily after your baby is born. This is caused by changes in your
hormones, lack of sleep and the hard work of adjusting to a newborn baby. This is called the Baby Blues. You will usually feel better in a week or so.
If the feelings last longer or get worse, you may be having postpartum depression.
If you have any of these symptoms you should call your doctor right away: · changes in your mood from very happy to very sad
· feeling irritable or cranky
· sleeping too little or too much
· thoughts of hurting yourself or your baby
It is important that you reach out for help. Call your doctor right away.
Point: Expected Psychological Changes (Resolved)
This happens right away for some but may take longer for others. This is normal.
''Baby blues'' are common during the first few weeks after delivery, caused in part by hormone changes, sleep deprivation and
family lifestyle adjustment. Blues usually resolve within a week after delivery. Postpartum depression is more serious and longer lasting. It is treatable. It is important to seek help. Symptoms of a problem should be reported to the care provider immediately. These include: - mood swings
- extreme anxiety
- sleep disturbances
- repetitive and intrusive thoughts
- worry about infant's welfare
- thoughts of harming self or infant - loss of interest in caring for infant - fear of being left alone with infant
Encourage Identification of a personal support system. Provide information on local postpartum depression support group or behavioral health resource.
Acceptance, Explanation, Verbalizes Understanding by AC at 1/31/2021 0640
Description:
During the postpartum period parents begin bonding or feeling attached to their infant.
Patient Friendly Description:

[DOB-997] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
You can also call a family member or friend for support. Consider counseling and joining a support group for parents with postpartum depression in your community.
Topic: Elsevier Education Documents (Resolved)
Point: VTE Prevention (Resolved)
To prevent VTE, early frequent ambulation is encouraged and should be continued at home. Provider's instructions for continuing VTE prophylaxis medication (as applicable) should be followed at home.
Point: Incision Care (Resolved)
Hands should be washed with soap and water before and after caring for incision. Generally, the dressing will be removed after 24 hours. The incision should be kept clean and dry by cleansing with soap and water, rinsing and patting dry to prevent infection. Taking a shower or soaking in a tub should be avoided until provider says that it is okay. Underwear or clothing that rubs or places pressure on the area should be avoided. The incision should be exposed to air as much as possible, particularly if it located under a skin flap.
Point: Cesarean Delivery, Care After (Resolved)

[DOB-999] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Title: Labor Education (Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Overview (Resolved)
Symptoms of labor may include: · pain that starts as a backache
· labor pains that are spaced evenly apart · some pink or light red mucus discharge from the birth canal (bloody show) · water breaks
You will notice that the labor pain gets stronger, lasts longer and gets closer together. Just before delivery, you may feel pressure and want to push (bear down).
Point: Description (Resolved)
First stage: - begins with regular uterine contractions and ends with complete cervical dilatation at 10 cm
Second stage: - begins with complete cervical dilatation and ends with the delivery of the infant
Third stage: - period between the delivery of the infant and the delivery of the placenta and fetal membranes
Point: Signs/Symptoms (Resolved)
- regular contractions that increase in intensity, frequency and duration
- passing of cervical mucus plug, bloody vaginal discharge (bloody show)
- discomfort usually beginning as a dull ache in lower back
- rupture of amniotic membranes
- pelvic pressure with the urge to push
Description:
Labor is the process during which the fetus, membranes, umbilical cord and placenta are expelled from the uterus. There
are 3 stages.

[DOB-1001] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
Topic: When to Seek Medical Attention (Resolved)
Point: Labor Care (Resolved)
If there is a birth plan, it should be reviewed with the patient and family. Contractions will be monitored throughout the labor process. Fetal status will be monitored frequently using auscultation or electronic fetal monitoring. Patient status will be assessed on an ongoing basis. Potential complications, such as bleeding or blood pressure elevation, should be addressed promptly. Control of pain during labor may be accomplished with a variety of methods (e.g., relaxation and breathing techniques, position
changes, analgesia or anesthesia). As labor progresses, position changes can be expected.
If there are positions that will better facilitate delivery and are not contraindicated, they should be utilized. Support during labor should be encouraged.
Point: Inform Healthcare Team (Resolved)
- increasing pain/discomfort - rupture of membranes
- urge to push

[DOB-1005] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
Title: Breastfeeding Education (Adult,Obstetrics) ()
Topic: Self-Management (Resolved)
You will make more milk if you feed your baby many times a day. Breastfeed your baby whenever they act hungry (feeding on demand). Let your baby feed from both breasts for as long as they want (baby-led feeding schedule). Your baby may be very sleepy right after they are born (first 2 weeks). You may need to wake them up for
feeding. New babies: · will need to be breastfed every 1 to 3 hours
Point: Pacifiers and Formula (Resolved)
Nonnutritive sucking with a pacifier may benefit the preterm infant. Milk substitutes (formula) can lead to decreased breast milk supply and alter the benefits of breast milk for the infant.
Point: Infant-Led Feeding (Resolved)
The infant should be breastfed: - when they show signs of being hungry (feeding on demand)
- to reduce the fullness of the lactating breasts
- until they are done (infant-led feeding schedule)
The infant should be allowed to feed on each breast for as long as they want.
In the first few weeks following birth, the infant may be sleepier and need to be awakened for a feeding. As a guide: - newborns may breastfeed every 1 to 3 hours
- newborns should not go longer than 3 hours without a feeding during the day
- newborns should not go longer than 5 hours without a feeding during the night - infants should be fed at least 8 times in a 24 hour period
0742

Patient Education (continued) · should not wait longer than 3 hours before being fed during the day
· should not wait longer than 5 hours before being fed during the night
Babies should be fed at least 8 times a day (within 24 hours). Point: Breast Care (Resolved)
To prevent nipples from becoming dry, cracked and sore, the patient should: - wash breasts every day
- avoid using soap on nipples
- air-dry nipples for 3 to 4 minutes after a feeding
- wear a supportive bra
- use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaking breast milk
- use lanolin or expressed breast milk on nipples after feeding to keep them from getting too dry
- ask their healthcare provider before using breast creams or breast shells
Point: Breast Milk Supply (Resolved)
- drink plenty of fluids and eat 3 healthy meals each day
- use breast massage and manual expression (before pumping or breastfeeding)
- pump frequently if unable to breastfeed; 8 times a day (every 2 to 3 hours during the day and at least once at night)
- avoid limiting the length of time for each breastfeeding session
Point: Infant Positioning (Resolved)

[DOB-1009] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
Patient Education (continued)
You can tell if your baby is getting enough to eat by counting their wet and dirty diapers. Age 3 to 4 days: · at least 3 to 5 wet diapers every day
· at least 2 dirty diapers every day
Age 5 to 7 days: · at least 4 wet diapers every day
· at least 3 to 4 dirty diapers every day
Pillows or blanket rolls should be used for support. The infant should be brought to the breast rather than leaning the breast forward toward the infant. Assist the breastfeeding parent in determining the most comfortable and effective hold for them and their infant (e.g., clutch or
football, cradle or across the lap, side-lying, ventral or lying down).
Point: Adequate Intake (Resolved)
Age 3 to 4 days of life: - infant having at least 3 to 5 wet diapers and at least 2 stools per 24 hours
Age 5 to 7 days of life: - infant having at least 4 wet diapers and at least 3 to 4 stools per 24 hours
Point: Latching/Swallowing (Resolved)
They may hear the infant swallowing between sucks (every 3 to 4 sucks). They will see muscle movement above and in front of the infant's ears while they are sucking. There should not be clicking or smacking while the infant is sucking. There should be no nipple pain while the infant is sucking.

Other Instructions
Discharge Instructions
Nothing vaginally until after seen in office. Bath, shower, stairs ok. Driving is ok when the baby is a week old and if you are not taking
percocet/narcotics. No intentional exercise or heavy lifting over 10 lbs. If something hurts, don't do it. Call for heavy vaginal bleeding more
than soaking a pad/hour, fever of 101. Follow up with Women First in 6 weeks. The office should contact you to make appointment within a
week of discharge.

[DOB-1051] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information
Breastfeeding
Choosing to breastfeed is one of the best decisions you can make for yourself and your 
baby. A change in hormones during pregnancy causes your breasts to make breast milk 
in your milk-producing glands. Hormones prevent breast milk from being released 
before your baby is born. They also prompt milk flow after birth. Once breastfeeding 
has begun, thoughts of your baby, as well as his or her sucking or crying, can stimulate the release of milk from your 
milk-producing glands.
Benefits of breastfeeding
Research shows that breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infants and mothers. It also offers a cost-free and 
convenient way to feed your baby.
For your baby
· Your first milk (colostrum) helps your baby's digestive system to function better. · Special cells in your milk (antibodies) help your baby to fight off infections. · Breastfed babies are less likely to develop asthma, allergies, obesity, or type 2 diabetes. They are also at lower 
risk for sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS). · Nutrients in breast milk are better able to meet your baby’s needs compared to infant formula. · Breast milk improves your baby's brain development.
For you
· Breastfeeding helps to create a very special bond between you and your baby. · Breastfeeding is convenient. Breast milk costs nothing and is always available at the correct temperature. · Breastfeeding helps to burn calories. It helps you to lose the weight that you gained during pregnancy. · Breastfeeding makes your uterus return faster to its size before pregnancy. It also slows bleeding (lochia) after 
you give birth. · Breastfeeding helps to lower your risk of developing type 2 diabetes, osteoporosis, rheumatoid arthritis, 
cardiovascular disease, and breast, ovarian, uterine, and endometrial cancer later in life.
Breastfeeding basics
Starting breastfeeding
· Find a comfortable place to sit or lie down, with your neck and back well-supported. · Place a pillow or a rolled-up blanket under your baby to bring him or her to the level of your breast (if you are 
seated). Nursing pillows are specially designed to help support your arms and your baby while you breastfeed. · Make sure that your baby's tummy (abdomen) is facing your abdomen. · Gently massage your breast. With your fingertips, massage from the outer edges of your breast inward toward the 
nipple. This encourages milk flow. If your milk flows slowly, you may need to continue this action during the 
feeding. · Support your breast with 4 fingers underneath and your thumb above your nipple (make the letter "C" with your 
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 423

[DOB-1053] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued)
hand). Make sure your fingers are well away from your nipple and your baby’s mouth. · Stroke your baby's lips gently with your finger or nipple. · When your baby's mouth is open wide enough, quickly bring your baby to your breast, placing your entire nipple 
and as much of the areola as possible into your baby's mouth. The areola is the colored area around your nipple.
○ More areola should be visible above your baby's upper lip than below the lower lip.
○ Your baby's lips should be opened and extended outward (flanged) to ensure an adequate, comfortable 
latch.
○ Your baby's tongue should be between his or her lower gum and your breast. · Make sure that your baby's mouth is correctly positioned around your nipple (latched). Your baby's lips should 
create a seal on your breast and be turned out (everted). · It is common for your baby to suck about 2–3 minutes in order to start the flow of breast milk. Latching
Teaching your baby how to latch onto your breast properly is very important. An improper latch can cause nipple pain, 
decreased milk supply, and poor weight gain in your baby. Also, if your baby is not latched onto your nipple properly, 
he or she may swallow some air during feeding. This can make your baby fussy. Burping your baby when you switch 
breasts during the feeding can help to get rid of the air. However, teaching your baby to latch on properly is still the 
best way to prevent fussiness from swallowing air while breastfeeding.
Signs that your baby has successfully latched onto your nipple
· Silent tugging or silent sucking, without causing you pain. Infant's lips should be extended outward (flanged). · Swallowing heard between every 3–4 sucks once your milk has started to flow (after your let-down milk reflex
occurs). · Muscle movement above and in front of his or her ears while sucking.
Signs that your baby has not successfully latched onto your nipple
· Sucking sounds or smacking sounds from your baby while breastfeeding. · Nipple pain.
If you think your baby has not latched on correctly, slip your finger into the corner of your baby’s mouth to break the
suction and place it between your baby's gums. Attempt to start breastfeeding again.
Signs of successful breastfeeding
Signs from your baby
· Your baby will gradually decrease the number of sucks or will completely stop sucking. · Your baby will fall asleep. · Your baby's body will relax. · Your baby will retain a small amount of milk in his or her mouth. · Your baby will let go of your breast by himself or herself.
Signs from you
· Breasts that have increased in firmness, weight, and size 1–3 hours after feeding. · Breasts that are softer immediately after breastfeeding. · Increased milk volume, as well as a change in milk consistency and color by the fifth day of breastfeeding. · Nipples that are not sore, cracked, or bleeding.
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 424

[DOB-1055] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued)
Signs that your baby is getting enough milk
· Wetting at least 1–2 diapers during the first 24 hours after birth. · Wetting at least 5–6 diapers every 24 hours for the first week after birth. The urine should be clear or pale yellow
by the age of 5 days. · Wetting 6–8 diapers every 24 hours as your baby continues to grow and develop. · At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 5 days. The stool should be soft and yellow. · At least 3 stools in a 24-hour period by the age of 7 days. The stool should be seedy and yellow. · No loss of weight greater than 10% of birth weight during the first 3 days of life. · Average weight gain of 4–7 oz (113–198 g) per week after the age of 4 days. · Consistent daily weight gain by the age of 5 days, without weight loss after the age of 2 weeks.
After a feeding, your baby may spit up a small amount of milk. This is normal.
Breastfeeding frequency and duration
Frequent feeding will help you make more milk and can prevent sore nipples and extremely full breasts (breast 
engorgement). Breastfeed when you feel the need to reduce the fullness of your breasts or when your baby shows signs 
of hunger. This is called "breastfeeding on demand." Signs that your baby is hungry include: · Increased alertness, activity, or restlessness. · Movement of the head from side to side. · Opening of the mouth when the corner of the mouth or cheek is stroked (rooting). · Increased sucking sounds, smacking lips, cooing, sighing, or squeaking. · Hand-to-mouth movements and sucking on fingers or hands. · Fussing or crying.
Avoid introducing a pacifier to your baby in the first 4-6 weeks after your baby is born. After this time, you may 
choose to use a pacifier. Research has shown that pacifier use during the first year of a baby's life decreases the risk of 
sudden infant death syndrome (SIDS).
Allow your baby to feed on each breast as long as he or she wants. When your baby unlatches or falls asleep while 
feeding from the first breast, offer the second breast. Because newborns are often sleepy in the first few weeks of life, 
you may need to awaken your baby to get him or her to feed.
Breastfeeding times will vary from baby to baby. However, the following rules can serve as a guide to help you make 
sure that your baby is properly fed: · Newborns (babies 4 weeks of age or younger) may breastfeed every 1–3 hours. · Newborns should not go without breastfeeding for longer than 3 hours during the day or 5 hours during the night. · You should breastfeed your baby a minimum of 8 times in a 24-hour period.
Breast milk pumping
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 425

Baptist Health Louisville
4000 KRESGE WAY
Louisville KY 40207-4605

[DOB-1057] - Admission (Discharged) in BAPTIST HEALTH LOUISVILLE 3 SOUTH (continued)
More Information (continued)
Pumping and storing breast milk allows you to make sure that your baby is exclusively fed your 
breast milk, even at times when you are unable to breastfeed. This is especially important if you go 
back to work while you are still breastfeeding, or if you are not able to be present during feedings. 
Your lactation consultant can help you find a method of pumping that works best for you and give 
you guidelines about how long it is safe to store breast milk.
Caring for your breasts while you breastfeed
Nipples can become dry, cracked, and sore while breastfeeding. The following recommendations can help keep your 
breasts moisturized and healthy: · Avoid using soap on your nipples. · Wear a supportive bra designed especially for nursing. Avoid wearing underwire-style bras or extremely tight 
bras (sports bras). · Air-dry your nipples for 3–4 minutes after each feeding. · Use only cotton bra pads to absorb leaked breast milk. Leaking of breast milk between feedings is normal. · Use lanolin on your nipples after breastfeeding. Lanolin helps to maintain your skin's normal moisture barrier. 
Pure lanolin is not harmful (not toxic) to your baby. You may also hand express a few drops of breast milk and 
gently massage that milk into your nipples and allow the milk to air-dry.
In the first few weeks after giving birth, some women experience breast engorgement. Engorgement can make your 
breasts feel heavy, warm, and tender to the touch. Engorgement peaks within 3–5 days after you give birth. The
following recommendations can help to ease engorgement: · Completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding or pumping. You may want to start by applying warm, moist 
heat (in the shower or with warm, water-soaked hand towels) just before feeding or pumping. This increases 
circulation and helps the milk flow. If your baby does not completely empty your breasts while breastfeeding, 
pump any extra milk after he or she is finished. · Apply ice packs to your breasts immediately after breastfeeding or pumping, unless this is too uncomfortable for 
you. To do this:
○ Put ice in a plastic bag.
○ Place a towel between your skin and the bag.
○ Leave the ice on for 20 minutes, 2–3 times a day. · Make sure that your baby is latched on and positioned properly while breastfeeding.
If engorgement persists after 48 hours of following these recommendations, contact your health care provider or a 
lactation consultant.
Overall health care recommendations while breastfeeding
· Eat 3 healthy meals and 3 snacks every day. Well-nourished mothers who are breastfeeding need an additional 
450–500 calories a day. You can meet this requirement by increasing the amount of a balanced diet that you eat. · Drink enough water to keep your urine pale yellow or clear. · Rest often, relax, and continue to take your prenatal vitamins to prevent fatigue, stress, and low vitamin and 
mineral levels in your body (nutrient deficiencies). · Do not use any products that contain nicotine or tobacco, such as cigarettes and e-cigarettes. Your baby may be 
harmed by chemicals from cigarettes that pass into breast milk and exposure to secondhand smoke. If you need 
help quitting, ask your health care provider.
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 426

More Information (continued) · Avoid alcohol. · Do not use illegal drugs or marijuana. · Talk with your health care provider before taking any medicines. These include over-the-counter and 
prescription medicines as well as vitamins and herbal supplements. Some medicines that may be harmful to your 
baby can pass through breast milk. · It is possible to become pregnant while breastfeeding. If birth control is desired, ask your health care provider 
about options that will be safe while breastfeeding your baby.
Where to find more information:
La Leche League International: www.llli.org
Contact a health care provider if: · You feel like you want to stop breastfeeding or have become frustrated with breastfeeding. · Your nipples are cracked or bleeding. · Your breasts are red, tender, or warm. · You have:
○ Painful breasts or nipples.
○ A swollen area on either breast.
○ A fever or chills.
○ Nausea or vomiting.
○ Drainage other than breast milk from your nipples. · Your breasts do not become full before feedings by the fifth day after you give birth. · You feel sad and depressed. · Your baby is:
○ Too sleepy to eat well.
○ Having trouble sleeping.
○ More than 1 week old and wetting fewer than 6 diapers in a 24-hour period.
○ Not gaining weight by 5 days of age. · Your baby has fewer than 3 stools in a 24-hour period. · Your baby's skin or the white parts of his or her eyes become yellow.
Get help right away if: · Your baby is overly tired (lethargic) and does not want to wake up and feed. · Your baby develops an unexplained fever.
Summary
· Breastfeeding offers many health benefits for infant and mothers. · Try to breastfeed your infant when he or she shows early signs of hunger. · Gently tickle or stroke your baby's lips with your finger or nipple to allow the baby to open his or her mouth. 
Bring the baby to your breast. Make sure that much of the areola is in your baby's mouth. Offer one side and burp 
the baby before you offer the other side. · Talk with your health care provider or lactation consultant if you have questions or you face problems as you 
breastfeed.

Breast Pumping Tips
Breast pumping is a way to get milk out of your breasts. You will then store the milk for your baby to use when you are 
away from home. There are three ways to pump. You can: · Use your hand to massage and squeeze your breast (hand expression). · Use a hand-held machine to manually pump your milk. · Use an electric machine to pump your milk.
In the beginning you may not get much milk. After a few days your breasts should make more. Pumping can help you 
start making milk after your baby is born. Pumping helps you to keep making milk when you are away from your baby.
When should I pump?
You can start pumping soon after your baby is born. Follow these tips: · When you are with your baby:
○ Pump after you breastfeed.
○ Pump from the free breast while you breastfeed. · When you are away from your baby:
○ Pump every 2–3 hours for 15 minutes. ○ Pump both breasts at the same time if you can. · If your baby drinks formula, pump around the time your baby gets the formula. · If you drank alcohol, wait 2 hours before you pump. · If you are going to have surgery, ask your doctor when you should pump again.
How do I get ready to pump?
Take steps to relax. Try these things to help your milk come in: · Smell your baby's blanket or clothes. · Look at a picture or video of your baby. · Sit in a quiet, private space. · Massage your breast and nipple. · Place a cloth on your breast. The cloth should be warm and a little wet. · Play relaxing music. · Picture your milk flowing.
What are some tips?
General tips for pumping breast milk
· Always wash your hands before pumping. · If you do not get much milk or if pumping hurts, try different pump settings or a 
different kind of pump. · Drink enough fluid so your pee (urine) is clear or pale yellow. · Wear clothing that opens in the front or is easy to take off. · Pump milk into a clean bottle or container.
Printed on 11/18/24 2:38 PM Page 429
